lesson_meta.title: THE MISTAKEN PLATE || தவறுதலாகக் கருதப்பட்ட தட்டு

sections.title: 1. Summary of the Entire Lesson
sections.content: The story 'The Mistaken Plate' revolves around two merchants who sold things made of brass and tin. They travelled across rivers and mountains, dividing the streets to avoid disturbing each other. One merchant was honest, while the other was greedy and always tried to pay as little as possible. In an old house, a poor grandmother (Ammama) and her granddaughter (Kanna) lived. They had a single plate left from their wealthy past, kept in memory of the grandfather. The greedy merchant was called to value the plate. He scratched it with a needle, realized it was pure gold, but lied, saying it was not even worth one rupee, and threw it on the ground to buy it cheaper later. Later, the honest merchant passed by. He respectfully examined the plate, truthfully informed them it was made of gold, and admitted he was not rich enough to buy it. When the grandmother offered to trade it, he gave them all his money, wares, donkey, and cart, keeping only eight rupees to pay the ferry boatman. When the greedy merchant returned offering ten rupees, he was shocked to learn the honest merchant had already bought it by paying a huge price. The story highlights that honesty brings true reward, while greed leads to loss.

sections.title: 2. Additional Areas and Notes
sections.content: Let Us Read Aloud (Supplementary Passage): This section contains a story about a poor but honest farmer who lived with his wife and seven children. He found a bag of gold in his field. Though his wife asked him to keep some, he believed 'honesty is the best policy' and returned the bag to its rightful rich owner, who thanked him. Let Us Write (Dialogue Writing): Introduces dialogue structure. Contains conversations between Miss Meena's students (Ravi and Glory) discussing a story project, and a conversation between Rathi and a Shopkeeper about buying a ten-rupee pen.

sections.title: 3. Points to Remember and One-Mark Areas
sections.content: Metals sold by merchants: Brass and Tin. True metal of the plate: Gold. Ferry charge given to the boatman: 8 rupees. Greedy merchant's first fake valuation: Not even 1 rupee. Greedy merchant's second offer: 10 rupees. Tool used to scratch the plate: A needle. Family members in the old house: Grandmother (Ammama) and Granddaughter (Kanna). Number of farmer's children: Seven. Price of the pen Rathi bought: 10 rupees.

sections.title: 4. Book Back Evaluation Exercises (Fully Covered)
sections.content: A. Choose the correct answer: 1. The merchants sold things made of brass. 2. The grandmother had kept the plate in memory of her husband. 3. In real the plate was made of gold. 4. The merchant paid eight rupees to the boatman. 5. Secondly the greedy merchant is ready to offer ten rupees. B. Whose words are these?: 1. 'Utensils made of brass and tin for sale!' - Both Merchants. 2. 'I am not rich enough to buy this plate. This is a gold plate.' - Good Merchant. 3. 'We do not have money to buy anything.' - Grandmother (Ammama). C. I Can Do (Vocabulary & Grammar): Synonyms: Foot hills - low hill at base; Kingdom - country. Animal Sounds: Monkey - chatter; Elephant - trumpet; Rooster - crow. Rhyming Words: say - day; go - grow; all - fall. Articles: the Himalayas; the Vaigai; an old man; the Sun.

questions.question: What kind of items did the two merchants sell?
options: Things made of gold and silver
Things made of wood and glass
Things made of brass and tin
Things made of iron and steel
questions.explanation: The story clearly states that the merchants sold things made of brass and tin.

questions.question: Why did the two merchants divide the streets of the town?
options: They did not want to disturb each other while selling
They were ordered by the king
They wanted to race each other
questions.explanation: They divided the streets so they wouldn't compete or disturb each other in the same area.

questions.question: Who lived in the old house?
questions.explanation: An old woman (grandmother) and her granddaughter lived in the old house in poverty.

questions.question: In whose memory did the grandmother keep the old plate?
questions.explanation: The text mentions, 'The grandmother had kept the plate in memory of her husband.'

questions.question: What affectionate name did the grandmother use to call her granddaughter?
questions.explanation: The grandmother addresses her granddaughter by saying, 'Kanna, we do not have money...'

questions.question: What did the greedy merchant use to scratch the old plate?
questions.explanation: The greedy man took the plate and scratched it with a needle to test the metal.

questions.question: What did the greedy merchant realize after scratching the plate?
options: It was a useless tin plate
questions.explanation: He found out the true value of the plate, which was that it was made of gold.

questions.question: What value did the greedy merchant verbally claim the plate had?
questions.explanation: To cheat them, he said, 'What is it worth? Not even one rupee.'

questions.question: What did the greedy merchant do immediately after lying about the plate's value?
options: He threw the plate on the ground and walked away
He kept it in his bag secretly
He gave them a brass vessel
questions.explanation: He threw it down and left, planning to return later and buy it for a very cheap price.

questions.question: Why did the granddaughter suggest showing the plate to the second merchant?
options: He looked nice and the first merchant was angry
She knew the plate was gold
questions.explanation: She said, 'Grandma, that merchant was angry. This merchant looks nice. Maybe we will get something.'

questions.question: What was the honest merchant's reaction upon realizing the plate was gold?
options: He said he was not rich enough to buy it
questions.explanation: He honestly told them, 'I am not rich enough to buy this plate. This is a gold plate.'

questions.question: What did the honest merchant give to the grandmother in exchange for the plate?
options: All his money, his donkey, his cart, and his wares
questions.explanation: He gave everything he had to trade fairly for the valuable gold plate.

questions.question: How much money did the honest merchant keep for himself?
questions.explanation: He only kept eight rupees for the ferry to go home.

questions.question: To whom did the honest merchant pay the eight rupees?
questions.explanation: He paid the eight rupees to the boatman to take him across the river.

questions.question: When the greedy merchant returned to the old house, how much did he offer for the plate?
questions.explanation: He called the girl and said, 'I've changed my mind. I will give you ten rupees for it.'

questions.question: Identify the speaker: 'Ammama, please buy something for me.'
questions.explanation: The granddaughter said this when she heard the merchant calling in the street.

questions.question: Identify the speaker: 'I am not rich enough to buy this plate. This is a gold plate.'
questions.explanation: The honest (second) merchant said this when he checked the plate.

questions.question: Identify the speaker: 'Are you sure? This is gold? The other merchant threw this on the ground...'
questions.explanation: The grandmother said this in shock when the honest merchant told her the truth.

questions.question: In the 'Let Us Read Aloud' section, what kind of person was the farmer?
questions.explanation: The passage begins with, 'There lived a poor but an honest farmer in a village.'

questions.question: How many children did the honest farmer have?
questions.explanation: The passage states he supported himself, his wife, and seven children.

questions.question: What did the farmer find while walking in his field?
questions.explanation: One day while he was walking in his field, he found a bag of gold.

questions.question: What did the farmer's wife suggest he do with the found item?
options: Return it to the owner immediately
questions.explanation: His wife told him to keep some of the gold, but he refused.

questions.question: What is the main idea of the story about the farmer?
questions.explanation: The farmer proved that 'honesty is the best policy' by returning the gold bag.

questions.question: In the dialogue writing section, what project did Miss Meena ask the class to do?
questions.explanation: The text says, 'Miss Meena has asked the class to write a story as a project.'

questions.question: In the shop dialogue, what does Rathi want to buy?
questions.explanation: Rathi visits the shop and asks the shopkeeper, 'I need a pen.'

questions.question: What is the meaning of the word 'Foot hills'?
questions.explanation: According to the 'I Can Do' section, Foot hills means 'low hill at base'.

questions.question: What is the meaning of the word 'Kingdom'?
questions.explanation: According to the vocabulary section, Kingdom means 'country'.

questions.question: Identify the correct sound made by a Monkey.
questions.explanation: Monkeys make a 'chatter' sound.

questions.question: Identify the correct sound made by an Elephant.
questions.explanation: Elephants make a 'trumpet' sound.

questions.question: Identify the correct sound made by a Rooster.
questions.explanation: Roosters make a 'crow' sound.

questions.question: Find the correct rhyming word for 'say'.
questions.explanation: Say rhymes with day.

questions.question: Find the correct rhyming word for 'go'.
questions.explanation: Go rhymes with grow.

questions.question: Find the correct rhyming word for 'all'.
questions.explanation: All rhymes with fall.

questions.question: Fill in the blank with the correct article: ____ Himalayas.
questions.explanation: We use 'The' before names of mountain ranges: The Himalayas.

questions.question: Fill in the blank with the correct article: ____ Vaigai.
questions.explanation: We use 'The' before names of rivers: The Vaigai.

questions.question: Fill in the blank with the correct article: ____ old man.
questions.explanation: 'Old' starts with a vowel sound, so we use 'An'.

questions.question: Fill in the blank with the correct article: ____ Sun.
questions.explanation: We use 'The' for unique objects in nature: The Sun.

questions.question: Match the characters with their actions: 1. Greedy Merchant A. Paid 8 rupees, 2. Good Merchant B. Scratched with a needle, 3. Boatman C. Received all wares.
questions.explanation: Greedy merchant scratched the plate. Good merchant gave all wares. Boatman was paid 8 rupees.

questions.question: Assertion (A): The greedy merchant threw the golden plate on the ground. Reason (R): He thought the plate was actually worthless and made of cheap tin.
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true, but R is false
A is false, but R is true
questions.explanation: He knew it was gold. He threw it to pretend it was worthless so he could buy it cheap later. So, Reason (R) is false.

questions.question: Analogy: Monkey : Chatter :: Rooster : ?
questions.explanation: Just as a monkey chatters, a rooster crows.

questions.question: Life Situation: You find a classmate's expensive pen on the floor. What is the most honest thing to do based on the lessons learned?
options: Keep it for yourself since you found it.
Sell it to another student for money.
Give it to the teacher or the rightful owner.
Leave it on the floor and walk away.
questions.explanation: Like the honest farmer and the good merchant, honesty dictates returning lost or valuable items to their rightful owners.

questions.question: Assertion (A): The honest merchant gave all his wares, cart, and money to the grandmother. Reason (R): He wanted to cheat her by giving useless things instead of cash.
options: Both A and R are true
A is true, but R is false
A is false, but R is true
Both A and R are false
questions.explanation: He gave everything because he knew the plate was very valuable and he wanted to be as fair as possible, not to cheat her.

questions.question: What was the greedy merchant's ultimate punishment for his dishonesty?
options: He lost the golden plate forever
questions.explanation: Because he lied and waited to get it cheaper, the good merchant bought it, leaving the greedy merchant with nothing.

questions.question: Match the prices with the correct context: 1. Ten Rupees A. Ferry charge, 2. Eight Rupees B. Fake valuation, 3. Zero Rupees C. Second offer.
questions.explanation: 10 rupees was the second offer. 8 rupees was the ferry charge. Zero (not even 1 rupee) was the fake valuation.

questions.question: Analogy: Good Merchant : Truthful :: Greedy Merchant : ?
questions.explanation: The good merchant told the truth, while the greedy merchant lied and deceived.

questions.question: Choose the correct sequence of events: A) The greedy merchant scratches the plate. B) The honest merchant pays his wares. C) The greedy merchant offers 10 rupees. D) The granddaughter asks to buy something.
questions.explanation: First the girl asks to buy. Greedy man checks and scratches it. Good man buys it. Greedy man returns with 10 rupees.

questions.question: What statement summarizes the farmer's belief when he found the bag of gold?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states: 'He told his wife that honesty is the best policy.'

questions.question: Who was always greedy as he tried to pay as less as possible?
options: The second merchant mentioned in the intro
questions.explanation: The story's introduction says, 'The second merchant was always greedy as he tried to pay as less as possible.'

questions.question: Life Situation: A shopkeeper mistakenly gives you more change than you deserve. Based on the lesson, what should you do?
options: Return the extra money to the shopkeeper
questions.explanation: Honesty requires returning what does not rightfully belong to you.

questions.question: Assertion (A): The grandmother and granddaughter lived in poverty. Reason (R): They only had one old plate left as a memory of their past wealth.
options: Both A and R are true, and R supports A
Both A and R are true, but R does not support A
A is true, but R is false
A is false, but R is true
questions.explanation: The fact that they only had one plate left from their wealthy past clearly illustrates their current state of poverty.
